children.text: You were just about to tell us how you got active with the Chicano activist movement?

Yes, and I was also at that time with Rosie Castro. They were asking for volunteers. I was right there at the Crystal City Boycott. Same thing, same principle, as Edgewood and Lenier and these guys, they really went on strike. These guys were asking- because they did not want the kids to lose touch with school. So because we were in education, and we were education majors, they asked for volunteers to go and spend the Christmas break- the month we were off, we were off a month- in Crystal City. And we went and José Ángel was there, I met Luz. And we worked with the kids! In this big old community center or hall, whatever, I'll never forget that, Because I think the girls had a house to themselves. Some of the people they had two houses, you know one for the guys and one for the girls. And we would go and teach these kids in this big community hall, you know. And we would play with them, you know, and we would read to them, and help them with their writing. And that's what we did. You know, we did that for a whole month.

Well the Raza- that when the Raza- they were organizing the Raza Unida Party. You know? And actually, Rosie became one of the Raza Unida candidates. Have you met with her yet? I don't know if you will. So anyway, you know, that’s how I started getting involved and going to all these functions and meetings, and all this. I started going to this place right there on Barkley and Castroville. It used to be, I don't know, I wanna say it was a barbershop, but I don't think it was a- I don't know what it was. But they would lend us the place. Somebody lived upstairs, that I know of. They would lend us this place and all these leaders would come and meet there and they called in Barrios Unidos, mmkay? It was Juan Patlán, he was the original head of the Mexican American Unity Counsel. They had MANO there, the Mexican American Neighborhood Association- (corrects herself) Organization. They had MAYO there, and they had the Theatro. That was with George Valasquez

And then, they had this little known- starting the Brown Berets. Okay, I used to go do volunteer there because Rosie told me they need volunteers. We use to go there supposedly like secretaries, you know, and do some filing and do some typing, and do some- whatever it was that these men wanted. And that's how I started going to these different functions and volunteering for fundraisers and things like this. I also went to a lot of the protests, you know. As a- as a citizen, protesting- not belonging to any one group just yet you know, just going as an individual protest, you know, for the rights. What attracted me to the Brown Berets is- I don't know. I went to their 16 de Septiembre celebration and they had a booth there and they were all there dressed like soldados. And something to me about the discipline they seemed to have at that time attracted me, you know. But, then I was dismayed that didn't have any women. They didn't have any women in the Brown Berets. And that where I met my compadre Jerry Erispe. I baptized his daughter. Actually, I just talked to him not that long ago. It was his birthday. Anyway um, I started hanging around with them, you know? With Jerry.

And I met some of the other Berets and actually became- I guess you want to call it “lover” or girlfriend to one of 'em. I'm not going to say his name. But that was pretty hot and heave for a while, but it really had nothing to do with my commitment to the Raza. And finally, I approached Juan Guarjado and you know, we talked. You know, we talked about the possibilities of opening up a women’s chapter. But my job was not done because he says "you need to bring some women that are truly committed." Because they were like soldiers, that's what they were. And he explained to me what it was and I was like "yeah." I was all militant. It was almost like I was gonna join the Chicano version of the Weather Underground, you know, movement. "We're gonna blow up things, and you know really go back to Malcolm X- by any means necessary." So, I was- I was you know, ready. And my mind was there, you know. And I honestly had a little bit of hard time rounding up women that wanted to go that route, but I did. Two of them were from Del Rio, my hometown. One of them was a former roommate- Silvia Hernandez and Letty Gallegos. You know, they came- they wanted to be Brown Berets. Letty actually made the Army her career.

The Crystal City Boycott took place in the 1969-70 school year in Crystal City, Texas. The conflict started when Mexican American students were excluded from the Crystal City High School cheerleading team, but lead to a protest against Mexican American discrimination in schools overall, and expanded to include students from the middle and elementart schools as well. Over 200 students struck with the support of their families, demanding a bilingual and bicultural education and less discriminatory testing. The boycott was successful.

José Ángel Gutiérrez was one of the three founders of the Mexican American Youth Organization (MAYO), which was formed in San Antonio in 1967. He was a former student of Crystal City High School and graduated from there in 1962.

The Mexican American Unity Council (MAUC) was established in 1964 as an economic development nonprofit organization. As a nonprofit, it could recieve grants from foundations, which it aimed to use to combat poverty and aid small businesses in the Mexican American community. It continues to exist today.

Weather Underground was a militant organization that operated in the United States from 1969 to 1977. They had a goal of overthrowing the U.S. government, which it belived to be imperialist. It carried out several bombings that were organized in retaliation to U.S. foreign affairs actions, including the invasion of Laos, the bombings in Hanoi, and the coup d'etat in Chile. It slowly faded after the U.S. pulled out of Vietnam in 1973, as opposing the Vietnam War was one of the main reasons for the organization's existance, along with Black Power and Marxism.

The Brown Berets were a Chicano paramilitary organization the opperated from 1967 to 1972 and were formed in Los Angeles, California. They were modeled after the Black Panthers, and their primary mission was to combat police brutality and promote Chicanismo, or self-determination for Chicanos.
